exemplar.title: Picture This: Investigating the Effect of Contextual Prerequisites on Comprehension and Recall

detailedExemplarData.title: Picture This: Investigating the Effect of Contextual Prerequisites on Comprehension and Recall

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

files.fileName: Giselle_khc988_Final Internal Assessment in Psychology HL.pdf

strengths: Clear and precise aim linked to Bransford & Johnsonâs study
Accurate description of schema theory and its connection to the investigation
weaknesses: Minor factual overstatement regarding the role of emotion in schema theory
Dependent variable not fully operationalized with a concrete scoring scheme
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction excels in stating a precise aim and situating it within schema theory (band-5 quality) but exhibits factual slip-ups and only partial operationalization of the DV. These shortcomings prevent a top-band (5â6) award, yet overall performance merits a strong mid-band mark of 4 out of 6.

strengths: Independent-measures design justified to prevent order effects
Thoughtful control of materials (hand-drawn image, AI narration)
weaknesses: Details of pilot testing (sample size, outcomes) are omitted
Explanation of how expectation bias is controlled is unclear
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section demonstrates a sound choice of design and material controls, but explanations remain surface-level, lacking depth on pilot findings and demand-characteristics controls. Performance aligns with lower mid-band (1â2), yielding 2 out of 4.

strengths: Appropriate use of Mann-Whitney U test with correct p-value interpretation
Findings are clearly linked to acceptance of the null hypothesis
weaknesses: Graphical presentations contain labeling and completeness errors
Data tables omit the no-context group and lack units/context
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical selection and interpretation are accurate and linked to the research question (top band), but presentation errors in graphs and incomplete tables limit clarity. This mixed performance merits a solid mid-band score of 3 out of 6.

strengths: Discussion effectively relates findings back to schema theory and original study
Limitations and modifications are explicitly linked and well justified
weaknesses: Alternative theoretical explanations are not fully explored
Cultural impact claims lack supporting evidence
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation demonstrates coherent discussion of results in theoretical context and well-reasoned modifications (band-5 qualities), yet would benefit from deeper consideration of alternative theories and substantiated cultural analysis. A score of 4 out of 6 reflects this strong but not flawless performance.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two bar charts are provided that plot means and standard deviations for each group, directly addressing the hypothesis. However, axes lack full labels/units and SD bars are awkwardly incorporated, constituting presentation errors. Therefore the work fits the âaddresses hypothesis but contains errorsâ descriptor.

The student explicitly states statistical findings (U=60.5, pâ.526), links them to acceptance of the null hypothesis, and ties this to the research question. Interpretation is logically connected to the data with no major flaws, fulfilling the top band though without the nuanced commentary that would warrant 6.

Both IV and DV are correctly identified inside the hypothesis section; however, operationalisation is only partial. The IV is operationalised (context image shown vs. no image) but the DV is described vaguely as âmemory of the passageâ without specifying the concrete scoring scheme (# of ideas recalled). This fulfils the 3-4 band rather than the 5-6 band.

Sampling (volunteer/self-select) is named, participant characteristics are detailed, and limited rationale (ease of access) is provided. This constitutes an explanation, albeit brief and convenience-focused, justifying mid-band (3-4).

Schema theory is accurately described, the original Bransford & Johnson study is summarised, and the student explains how the current investigation derives directly from that theory (activating schemas through context should enhance recall). Minor factual slips (e.g., overstating emotion in the cognitive approach) prevent award of the full 6, but the descriptionâconnection criterion is clearly met at a strong level.

Findings are compared to the original Bransford & Johnson results and situated within schema theory, exploring possible reasons for divergence. This discussion is coherent and clearly linked to background theory, consistent with the 5-6 band; minor depth limitations justify a 5.

The student states a precise aim â to replicate Bransford & Johnson (1972) in order to test whether providing context before a passage affects comprehension and recall â and explicitly explains why this aim is relevant (testing transferability, dependability, and credibility of the original findings within a school sample). The connection between the aim and real-world or theoretical significance is clearly articulated, though not deeply elaborated, warranting placement high in the 5-6 band but not perfect.

The student identifies multiple relevant limitations (sample size, homogeneity, altered materials, demand characteristics) and at least one strength (controlled presentation). Limitations are explained and explicitly related to how they might have affected results, meeting the âexplained and relevantâ descriptor at band 5-6.

Controlled variables (same exposure time, AI voice, identical recall period) and materials choices (projector, black-and-white drawing, AI audio) are described with brief justifications (avoid bias, equal viewing). This moves beyond mere listing, meriting the âexplainedâ band, though depth is modest.

Suggested modifications (larger, more diverse sample; additional conditions) are directly linked to the earlier limitations and justified in terms of improving generalisability and fidelity to the original study, satisfying the requirement for explicit linkage and justification set out in the top band.

The student identifies an independent-measures design and explains why it was chosen (separate groups to prevent order effects). Controls such as withholding the aim and using AI narration are discussed, showing more than a bare description but still somewhat surface-level, earning the lower end of the âexplainedâ band.

Appropriate descriptive statistics (mean, SD) and an appropriate inferential test (Mann-Whitney U for two independent, small, non-parametric samples) are applied. Calculations, critical values, p-value and interpretation are shown and appear accurate despite minor typographical errors, satisfying accurate application within the 5-6 band but not demonstrating sophistication that would secure 6.

annotations.comment: The student overstates emotionâs role in the cognitive approach; emotion is not its primary focus. Refine this to emphasize core schema processes rather than affective influence.
The definition and description of schema theory is clear and shows accurate understanding of Piagetâs model in organizing new information.
The independent variable is stated but not fully operationalized (what exactly constitutes the âcontextâ image?). Define precise characteristics for replication.
The dependent variable is described vaguely as âmemory of the passage.â Include the scoring rubric (e.g., number of ideas recalled) to operationalize this measure.
The link to Bransford & Johnsonâs study is outlined but not explicit. State clearly how each procedural modification preserves the theoretical basis of schema activation.
The original Bransford & Johnson design is misreported as three conditions. The original had five. Ensure factual accuracy when summarizing foundational studies.
Good explanation for simplifying to two conditions for classroom feasibility. Consider discussing how this change might affect internal validity.
Selfâselective sampling may introduce volunteer bias. Discuss its impact on generalizability or consider alternative sampling methods.
The control of expectation bias via âcaseâcontrol designâ is unclear. Clarify how participant awareness is prevented and link directly to demand characteristics.
Using a handâdrawn, uncolored image shown via projector to control visual bias demonstrates thoughtful control of materials.
Justification for independentâmeasures design to prevent order effects is wellâexplained and aligns with exploration design criteria.
Pilot testing is mentioned but lacking details on sample size and results. Summarize pilot outcomes to justify memorization timing.
Required materials omit specifics of the context image (e.g., resolution, content). Provide full details to ensure reproducibility.
The stepwise procedure is clear but the list formatting is inconsistent (nested lists). Standardize numbering and indentation for clarity.
Ethical considerations are thorough, covering informed consent, anonymity, withdrawal rights, and debriefing to meet IB ethical standards.
Table 1 headings lack units or context (e.g., specify maximum recall score). Add clear labels and units.
The interpretation correctly notes nonâsignificant differences and variability, linking findings to descriptive statistics.
Table 2 presents only the prior context group; data for the noâcontext group are omitted. Ensure complete data presentation.
Reâpresenting difficulty ratings under the same table label is misleading. Use consistent numbering (e.g., 'Table 3') to prevent confusion.
Linking high mean rating and lower SD to consistency is wellâarticulated, demonstrating correct interpretation of variability.
The zâscore table lacks critical value comparisons or pâvalue thresholds. Include these to clarify inferential conclusions.
Recommendations to increase conditions are relevant but would benefit from concrete examples (e.g., partial context group) to guide future designs.
The recommendation to diversify sampling techniques and increase sample size is wellâjustified and demonstrates thoughtful linkage to earlier limitations.
Correct the typo 'MannâwWhitney' in the inferential discussion to maintain professional presentation.
The interpretation effectively connects findings to encoding specificity and original study results, fulfilling evaluation discussion criteria.
Graph 1 lacks axis labels and error bars are not clearly distinguishable. Enhance formatting with labeled axes and legend.
Graph 2 presentation omits the noâcontext comparison bars and legend. Plot both groups sideâbyâside with clear labeling.
Evaluation restates results but could deepen discussion by considering alternative theoretical explanations beyond sampling and materials effects.
Cultural considerations are mentioned without evidence. Include references or participant data to support claims about cultural impact.
Limitations regarding sample size and homogeneity are clearly linked to impacts on validity, demonstrating highâlevel evaluation.
